context:
Section 8 (Conclusions), page 17 (arXiv:2207.09435v1)

Can we prove a better constant bound on the performance of the Offset\(\theta\) algorithm? The worst example we know is a factor of 2 (which follows from the results in Section 7).
title: Improve constant approximation bound for Offsetθ regret minimization under additive noise
background_latex:
Let there be a set of items \(N=\{1,2,\dots,n\}\). Each item \(i\) has an intrinsic value \(v_i\in\mathbb{R}\) (unknown to the algorithm) and an independent additive noise \(a_i\) drawn from a known distribution \(A_i\) with \(\mathbb{E}[a_i]=0\). The algorithm observes \(s_i := v_i + a_i\) and knows the noise distributions \(A:=(A_1,\dots,A_n)\).

An (possibly randomized) algorithm \(\mathrm{Alg}\) selects one item based on \((s,A)\). Let \(\mathrm{Alg}(s,A;v)\) denote the expected intrinsic value of the selected item when the intrinsic value vector is \(v\) and the observed vector is \(s=v+a\). The regret on a fixed value vector \(v\in\mathbb{R}^n\) is
\[
\mathrm{Reg}(\mathrm{Alg},v):= \max_{i\in N} v_i\; -\; \mathbb{E}_{a\sim A}\big[\mathrm{Alg}(v+a,A;v)\big].
\]
The worst-case regret is \(\mathrm{Reg}(\mathrm{Alg}) := \sup_{v\in\mathbb{R}^n} \mathrm{Reg}(\mathrm{Alg},v)\). For fixed noise distributions \(A\), define
\[
\mathrm{OPT}(A):=\inf_{\mathrm{Alg}} \mathrm{Reg}(\mathrm{Alg}).
\]

Fix a mapping \(\theta\) that assigns to each noise distribution \(D\) a real offset \(\theta(D)\). Define \(\theta_i:=\theta(A_i)\). The offset algorithm \(\mathrm{Offset}_\theta\) selects an index in
\[
\arg\max_{i\in N}\{s_i-\theta_i\}.
\]
A particular choice of \(\theta\) is defined by a minimax threshold rule in the binary case (two items) and then applied itemwise for general \(n\). For that specific \(\theta\), a universal constant \(C\) is proved such that \(\mathrm{Reg}(\mathrm{Offset}_\theta)\le C\,\mathrm{OPT}(A)\) for all \(n\) and all collections of independent mean-zero noises satisfying the paper's standing regularity assumption that, for each \(i\), the function \(\varphi_i(x):=\Pr_{a_i\sim A_i}(|a_i|\ge x)\cdot x\) is bounded over \(x\in[0,\infty)\).
problem_definition_latex:
For the specific offset mapping \(\theta\) defined by the minimax threshold rule in the binary case and applied itemwise, prove an improved universal constant-factor guarantee
\[
\mathrm{Reg}(\mathrm{Offset}_\theta)\le c\,\mathrm{OPT}(A)
\]
with some constant \(c\) strictly smaller than the currently proved value (\(c=57000\)), uniformly over all numbers of items \(n\) and all collections of independent mean-zero noise distributions \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_n)\) satisfying that each \(\varphi_i(x)=\Pr_{a_i\sim A_i}(|a_i|\ge x)\cdot x\) is bounded on \([0,\infty)\).
latex:
Let there be a set of items \(N=\{1,2,\dots,n\}\). Each item \(i\) has an intrinsic value \(v_i\in\mathbb{R}\) (unknown to the algorithm) and an independent additive noise \(a_i\) drawn from a known distribution \(A_i\) with \(\mathbb{E}[a_i]=0\). \[

\]
with some constant \(c\) strictly smaller than the currently proved value (\(c=57000\)), uniformly over all numbers of items \(n\) and all collections of independent mean-zero noise distributions \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_n)\) satisfying that each \(\varphi_i(x)=\Pr_{a_i\sim A_i}(|a_i|\ge x)\cdot x\) is bounded on \([0,\infty)\).
open_problem_quote: Can we prove a better constant bound on the performance of the Offset\(\theta\) algorithm? The worst example we know is a factor of 2 (which follows from the results in Section 7).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Searching for tight lower-bound instances for the approximation factor can be cast as generating noise distributions (e.g., discrete mixtures) and adversarial value vectors and numerically estimating \(\mathrm{Reg}(\mathrm{Offset}_\theta)/\mathrm{OPT}(A)\). However, computing \(\mathrm{OPT}(A)\) exactly is itself an infinite-dimensional optimization over policies, so automated searches would likely rely on restricted policy classes or relaxations, limiting conclusiveness.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop can explore families of noise distributions and value instances and attempt to (i) find larger empirical gaps (lower bounds) or (ii) experiment with proof-inspired inequalities to tighten constants in the existing analysis. The fitness signal can be an estimated gap using numerical optimization over a parameterized policy class, but faithfully capturing \(\mathrm{OPT}(A)\) remains challenging, making progress suggestive rather than definitive.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis: No relevant Scholar-organic forward citations were available for this problem. The forward-citation review therefore cannot identify follow-up work that resolves or materially advances the problem.
